title: The History of Soccer and the World Cup: A Short Introduction
description:
in History, Sports |   	June 22nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Every four years, humanôÙiôÙty underôÙgoes a great increase in its numôÙber of socôÙce
body:
in History, Sports |   	June 22nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Every four years, humanôÙiôÙty underôÙgoes a great increase in its numôÙber of socôÙcer fans ã or rather, footôÙball fans, dependôÙing on what part of the world weãre talkôÙing about. Thatãs not to imply that the world othôÙerôÙwise sufôÙfers from a dearth of enthuôÙsiôÙasts of that parôÙticôÙuôÙlar sport. Nor is footôÙball an obscure secôÙondary term: the lanôÙguage of most every counôÙtry obsessed with the thing itself has localôÙized that name for it, resultôÙing in a variôÙety of words from fû¤tôÙbol to futôÙbol to futeôÙbol to Fuûball. There remains the matôÙter of calôÙcio, but then, ItalôÙians have always done things their own way. So do AmerôÙiôÙcans, as this yearãs World Cup has emphaôÙsized, but youãll find that socôÙcer actuôÙalôÙly turns out not to have origôÙiôÙnatôÙed as yet anothôÙer awkôÙward cusôÙtom excluôÙsive to the UnitôÙed States.
In fact, it derives from a few letôÙters of the full British name of the game, ãassoôÙciôÙaôÙtion footôÙball.ã ComôÙmonôÙly heard in the U.K. up until the nineôÙteen-sevôÙenôÙties, socôÙcer evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly came in handy on the othôÙer side of the pond to difôÙferôÙenôÙtiôÙate it from what most of the world calls ãAmerôÙiôÙcan footôÙball.ã
As explained in about 20 minôÙutes in the Geo HisôÙtoôÙry video at the top of the post, the hisôÙtoôÙry of socôÙcer, footôÙball, fû¤tôÙbol, or whatôÙevôÙer you may call it is full of facts that will sureôÙly surôÙprise those of who only pay it any attenôÙtion when the World Cup comes around ã and may occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly surôÙprise the die-hards who live and breathe the game even durôÙing the off years. For a much deepôÙer (and more humorôÙous) dive into a narôÙrowôÙer slice of the past, we also have this two-hour hisôÙtoôÙry of the World Cupô from footôÙball YouTuôÙber Vizeh.


	

If you want to avoid a name speôÙcifôÙic to any one nationôÙal lanôÙguage, you can always refer to ãthe beauôÙtiôÙful game,ã but even if that adjecôÙtive applies to the action on the field, at least on a good day, it sits less easôÙiôÙly with theô polôÙiôÙtickôÙing, backôÙbitôÙing, and not-always-above-board dealôÙmakôÙing charôÙacôÙterôÙisôÙtic of its busiôÙness and adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion at a globôÙal scale. The whole enterôÙprise has come to repôÙreôÙsent all theô gloôÙries and ugliôÙness of moderôÙniôÙty, reduced to a rigidôÙly stanôÙdardôÙized batôÙtleôÙfieldô on which increasôÙingôÙly many nations of the world aspire toô achieve first presôÙence, then domôÙiôÙnaôÙtion. For examôÙple,ô South Korea, where I live, has made its seriôÙousôÙness on the pitch sufôÙfiôÙcientôÙly known over four straight decades of World Cup parôÙticôÙiôÙpaôÙtion thatô you might want to learn the KoreôÙan wordô chukgu ã at least if the comôÙing match with South Africa goes its way.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Restored Footage from the First World Cup: Uruguay, 1930
Pelûˋãs Great World Cup Goals (RIP)
Albert Camusã Lessons Learned from PlayôÙing Goalie: ãWhat I Know Most SureôÙly about MoralôÙiôÙty and ObligôÙaôÙtions, I Owe to FootôÙballã
The MonôÙty Python PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy SocôÙcer Match: The Ancient Greeks VerôÙsus the GerôÙmans
Why Jorge Luis Borges HatôÙed SocôÙcer: ãSocôÙcer is PopôÙuôÙlar Because StuôÙpidôÙiôÙty is PopôÙuôÙlarã
The Rules of 100 Sports ClearôÙly Explained in Short Videos: BaseôÙball, FootôÙball, Jai Alai, Sumo Wrestling, CrickôÙet, Pûˋtanque & Much More
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Encyclopedia of Women Philosophers: A New Web Site Presents the Contributions of Women Philosophers, from Ancient to Modern
description: In a conôÙverôÙsaôÙtion with Julian BagôÙgiôÙni on why there are so few women in acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy, Mary Warnock once notôÙed that ãof all the
body:
In a conôÙverôÙsaôÙtion with Julian BagôÙgiôÙni on why there are so few women in acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy, Mary Warnock once notôÙed that ãof all the humanôÙiôÙties departôÙments in British uniôÙverôÙsiôÙties, only phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy departôÙments have a mere 25% women memôÙbers.ã That numôÙber is even lowôÙer in the US. ãWhy should this be?ã Warnock asked. She assertôÙed that the probôÙlem may lie with the disôÙciôÙpline itself. ãI think that acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy has become an extraôÙorôÙdiôÙnarôÙiôÙly inward-lookôÙing subôÙject,ã she said, ãIf you pick up a proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal jourôÙnal now, you find litôÙtle nitôÙpickôÙing responsôÙes to preôÙviôÙous artiôÙcles. Women tend to get more easôÙiôÙly bored with this than men. PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy seems to stop being interôÙestôÙing just when it starts to be proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal.ã
Itãs a provocaôÙtive claim, one Iãm sure many women in phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy would conôÙtest, though the more genôÙerôÙal idea that acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy has become an arid pracôÙtice divorced from real life conôÙcerns might have wider supôÙport. The data on women in acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy presents a very comôÙplex picôÙture. ãNo sinôÙgle interôÙvenôÙtion is likeôÙly to change the cliôÙmate,ã as Tania LomôÙbroôÙzo writes at NPR. ExplicôÙit and implicôÙit biasôÙes do play a role, as do instances of sexôÙuôÙal harassôÙment and coerôÙcion by those in posiôÙtions of powôÙer. But anothôÙer sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant issue Warnock seemed to ignore is the way that phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy is genôÙerôÙalôÙly taught at the underôÙgradôÙuôÙate levôÙel.
In the research on which LomôÙbroôÙzo reports, studôÙies found that ãthe biggest drop in the proôÙporôÙtion of women in the phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy pipeline seems to be from enrollôÙment in an introôÙducôÙtoôÙry phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy class to becomôÙing a phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy major. At GeorôÙgia State, for examôÙple, women make up about 55 perôÙcent of IntroôÙducôÙtion to PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy stuôÙdents but only around 33 perôÙcent of phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy majors.ã This may have to do with the fact that ãreadôÙings on the sylôÙlabus were overôÙwhelmôÙingôÙly by men (over 89 perôÙcent).ã As GeorôÙgia State gradôÙuôÙate stuôÙdent MorôÙgan ThompôÙson explained at a conôÙferôÙence in 2013:
This probôÙlem is comôÙpoundôÙed by the fact that introôÙducôÙtoôÙry phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy textôÙbooks have an even worse genôÙder balôÙance; women account for only 6 perôÙcent of authors in a numôÙber of introôÙducôÙtoôÙry phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy textôÙbooks.
Does this disôÙparôÙiôÙty reflect an unalôÙterôÙable truth about the hisôÙtoôÙry of phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy? No, and it can very well be remeôÙdied. The CenôÙter for the HisôÙtoôÙry of Women PhilosoôÙphers and SciôÙenôÙtists is workôÙing to do that with a new site, the EncyôÙcloôÙpeôÙdia of ConôÙcise ConôÙcepts by Women PhilosoôÙphers. The joint project of PaderôÙborn Universityãs Ruth HagenôÙgruôÙber and CleveôÙland Stateãs Mary Ellen WaitôÙhe, this resource aims to introôÙduce ãwomen philosoôÙphers who mostôÙly have been omitôÙted from the philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal canon despite their hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal and philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal influôÙence.ãô So far, reports DaiôÙly Nous, ãthere are around 100 entriesãÎ with more to be added every few months.ã
Each entry is writôÙten by a recôÙogôÙnized scholôÙar. The easy-to-navôÙiôÙgate site has four main secôÙtions: ConôÙcepts, KeyôÙwords, PhilosoôÙphers, and ConôÙtribôÙuôÙtors. There are a few names most peoôÙple will recôÙogôÙnize, like Mary WollôÙstonecraft, Ayn Rand, and Simone de BeauôÙvoir. But most of these thinkers will seem obscure, despite their meanôÙingôÙful conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to varôÙiôÙous fields of thought. InteôÙgratôÙing these philosoôÙphers into sylôÙlabi and textôÙbooks could go a long way toward retainôÙing women in phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy departôÙments. As imporôÙtantôÙly, it will broadôÙen the traôÙdiôÙtion, givôÙing all stuôÙdents a wider range of perôÙspecôÙtives.
For examôÙple, much of the acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic work on social ethics in democôÙraôÙcy might refôÙerôÙence Adam Smithãs ãTheôÙoôÙry of Moral SenôÙtiôÙmentsã or the proôÙlifôÙic 20th cenôÙtuôÙry work of John Dewey. But it might overôÙlook the work of Deweyãs conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary Jane Addamsô (top), who also wrote critôÙiôÙcal studôÙies on democôÙraôÙcy and eduôÙcaôÙtion and who ãsees a conôÙnecôÙtion,ã writes MauôÙrice HamôÙingôÙton in a short entry about her, ãbetween symôÙpaôÙthetôÙic underôÙstandôÙing and a robust democôÙraôÙcy.ãÎ For Addams, it is cruôÙcial that citôÙiôÙzens in a democôÙraôÙcy engage with one anothôÙer to reach across difôÙferôÙence to care and find comôÙmon cause.ã
Addams brought her philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal conôÙcerns into real world pracôÙtice. She made imporôÙtant interôÙvenôÙtions in the treatôÙment of immiôÙgrants and African-AmerôÙiôÙcans in ChicaôÙgo, supôÙportôÙed workôÙing mothôÙers, and helped pass child proôÙtecôÙtion laws and end child labor. But while she has long been renowned as a social reformer and Nobel Peace Prize winôÙner, ãthe dynamôÙics of canon forôÙmaôÙtion,ã notes the StanôÙford EncyôÙcloôÙpeôÙdia of PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy, ãresultôÙed in her philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal work being largeôÙly ignored until the 1990s.ã Now, many philosoôÙphers recôÙogôÙnize that works likeô DemocôÙraôÙcy and Social Ethicsô anticôÙiôÙpatôÙed key conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary issues in politôÙiôÙcal phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy a cenôÙtuôÙry ago.
OthôÙer thinkers in theô EncyôÙcloôÙpeôÙdia of ConôÙcise ConôÙcepts by Women PhilosoôÙphersô like DioôÙtiôÙma of ManôÙtiôÙnea (whom Socrates revered) and earôÙly AmerôÙiôÙcan thinker MerôÙcy Otis WarôÙren made imporôÙtant conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions to the theôÙoôÙries of beauôÙty and govôÙernôÙment, respecôÙtiveôÙly. Yet they may receive no more than a footôÙnote in most underôÙgradôÙuôÙate phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy coursôÙes. This may have less to do with explicôÙit bias than with the way proôÙfesôÙsors themôÙselves have been eduôÙcatôÙed. But the hisôÙtoôÙry, and curôÙrent pracôÙtice, of phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy needs the incluôÙsion of these views. Learn more about many hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcalôÙly overôÙlooked women in phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphyô at the EncyôÙcloôÙpeôÙdia here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Meet Hûˋloû₤se, the Medieval Woman PhilosoôÙpher Who Turned a Doomed Love Affair into a MedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on Ethics
The ConôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions of Women PhilosoôÙphers RecovôÙered by the New Project Vox WebôÙsite
An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the FemôÙiôÙnist PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Simone de BeauôÙvoir
The Map of PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy: See All of the DisôÙciôÙplines, Areas & SubôÙdiôÙviôÙsions of PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy Mapped in a ComôÙpreôÙhenôÙsive Video
An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the FemôÙiôÙnist PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Simone de BeauôÙvoir
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: In Guadalajara, a Ceramic Tile Mural Mimics a Sun-Drenched Ecosystem
description: Thousands of handmade ceramic tiles nest together like a puzzle on the facade of the Torre San Luisô hotel in Guadalajara, Mexico. Abstract shapes
body:
Thousands of handmade ceramic tiles nest together like a puzzle on the facade of the Torre San Luisô hotel in Guadalajara, Mexico. Abstract shapes evocative of a lush garden ecosystem burst across the outdoor wall in a collaboration between Alex Proba and the artisans of CerûÀmica Suro.
Titled ãShape of Movement,ã the large-scale public work melds Probaãs organic visual language with a color palette that reflects the local environment. Earthy neutrals, alongside dusty pinks and blues, mimic the sun-drenched landscape, while the dynamic forms appear as if theyãre mid-motion.

ãThe work is about the movement we carry through spaces every day,ã Proba shares. ãI wanted the mural to feel as if the shapes are interacting with one another and flowing through the architecture itself.ã
This is the second collaboration between Proba and CerûÀmica Suro, following a vibrant swimming pool installation at a Miami home. Find more from both the artist and the studio on Instagram.

schema.headline: In Guadalajara, a Ceramic Tile Mural Mimics a Sun-Drenched Ecosystem

title: Willie Cole Traverses 'Mind, Body, and Soul' in Expressive Mixed Media Assemblages
description: For Willie Cole, the convergence of material and concept are as important as emotional and even spiritual links to history. Whether repurposing sa
body:
For Willie Cole, the convergence of material and concept are as important as emotional and even spiritual links to history. Whether repurposing salvaged musical instruments, creating enigmatic visages from stacked stilettos, or arranging hundreds of single-use plastic bottles across a surface, his imaginative sculptural assemblages tap into a range of global traditions, eras, and social and environmental issues.
Cole explores our associations with physical objects by removing them from the context within which weãre accustomed to encountering them. Time-honored African masking traditions and figurative sculptures made of high heels meet modern symbols of labor and culture, such as repeated ironing board motifs or a handful of saxophones that have been reimagined as a bird.
ãTiger Lillyã (2025), repurposed shoes and wire, 19 x 18 x 13 inches
Mind, Body, and Soul, Coleãs current solo exhibition at Sargentãs Daughters, combines new works with pieces made throughout the past couple of decades. The selection emphasizes the artistãs interest in reframing and enriching everyday objects into assemblages of cultural significance.
ãHis practice transcends specific media or subject matter, moving fluidly across genres and iconographies to explore history, consumerism, and environmentalism,ã the gallery says. In the era of fast fashion, the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, and an accelerating climate crisis, consumable and disposable objects contrast how we value our history and customs.
Mind, Body, and Soul continues through July 10 in New York. See more on the artistãs Instagram. 
ãFly Girlã (2016), repurposed shoes and wire, 18 x 12 x 14 inches
Alternate view of ãFly Girlã
ãSummer Shoesã (2025), silkscreen print, 21 x 27 inches
ãShoonufu Female Figureã (2013), bronze, 25 x 9.5 x 13 inches

Detail of ãSurvivors Series I (Wall Assemblages)ã
ãTwin Spiritsã (2026), repurposed shoes and wire, 27 x 12 x 11 inchea

title: David Daigle's Elaborate Punch-Cut Paper Pieces Excavate Commercial Imagery
description: Whether itãs a large-scale wallpaper reproduction of Sandro Botticelliãs ãThe Birth of Venusã or pages of deconstructed Artforum magazines, David
body:
Whether itãs a large-scale wallpaper reproduction of Sandro Botticelliãs ãThe Birth of Venusã or pages of deconstructed Artforum magazines, David Daigleãs detailed punch-cut compositions delve into the material and conceptual possibilities of layers, depth, and what is revealed or concealed.
Daigleãs forthcoming exhibition, The Death of Beauty at Track 16, investigates intersections of identity, consumer culture, and desire through a kind of sedimentary approach to commercial imagery, which he excavates with precise holes each revealing tiny tableaux. This method of dûˋcollage, which involves building up the surface and then removing elements, literally peels away the meanings and intentions behind contemporary visual culture.
Detail of ãThe Death of Venusã
ãThe Birth of Venusã serves as the foundational composition for Daigleãs ãThe Death of Venus,ã which has been completely overwhelmed by the artistãs hole-punched interventions. Tiny vignettes of people, things, and colorful dots completely erase and replace the titular figure. The masterpiece of 15th-century art has been reduced to an inexpensive, mass-market object for home decor and is further disarticulated by a sea of anonymous eyes and mouths. What could be more symbolic of our era of ultra consumerism?
Daigle digs into a wide array of photographs and printed media, from bus shelter advertisements to a panoramic photograph of Gaza taken around 2020, in which almost all of the buildings have been subsumed under the cratered surface. Itãs symbolic of the real devastation the city has experienced since the war began in late 2023. In a broader sense, the artistãs works reveal something akin to a visualization of the mediaãs often obscured undercurrent.
ãI am interested in sublimating technical images designed to generate desire,ã the artist says. ãThrough the subversive act of perforation, I search for the meanings trapped behind them. I want to see past the imagery, through the photograph itself, and ask whether media can become so untruthful that it ultimately consumes both itself and us.ã
The Death of Beauty opens on July 18 and continues through September 5 in East Hollywood. In the meantime, explore more on the artistãs Instagram.
ãGaza Cityã (2026), archival pigment print of Gaza City c. 2020 with layers of book pages relating to peace in the Middle East, 24 x 60 inches
Detail of ãGaza Cityã
Detail of ãGaza Cityã
ãBeigeã (2026), archival pigment print with Artforum magazines, 22 x 24 inches
ãSinead OãConnor (Herb Ritts Photo)ã (2026), Herb Ritts photo and Vogue magazine with punch-cut holes, 10 x 8 inches
Detail of ãThe Death of Venusã
Detail of ãThe Death of Venusã

title: In a New Documentary, Artists Get Candid About AI
description:
Film
                The short film from the Strategic National Arts Alumni Project is based on a survey of over 2,000 postsecon
body:
Film
                The short film from the Strategic National Arts Alumni Project is based on a survey of over 2,000 postsecondary arts graduates across the US. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        

One artist recalled a client asking her to recreate an AI-generated reference image in her style. Another was told to ãget over itã when she voiced hesitations about using generative technology. A motion graphic designer said he was certain heãd lose his job. An editorial cartoonist whose work is often political, based on his own upbringing in a dictatorship, observed that ãAI has no history, it has no soul.ã Still others said they embrace AI as a ãtool.ãIn a new short documentary from the Strategic National Arts Alumni Project (SNAAP), artists and creative workers get candid about generative technology, sharing their fears, ethical qualms, and real-life experiences. The 19-minute film, premiering exclusively on Hyperallergic, is based on the 2025 Pulse Survey on AI and Work, which collected responses from over 2,000 postsecondary arts and design graduates across more than 100 colleges and universities in the United States.The survey found that while 90% of respondents expressed skepticism about AI-generated output, including environmental, copyright, and privacy concerns, about half said they are already using it professionally. ãThat's the complex reality of a field navigating a major disruption in real time,ã said SNAAP Senior Research Fellow Joanna Woronkowicz. Perhaps the survey's most illuminating findings concern the unequal adoption of AI, and who benefits from its widespread use. As Woronkowicz and her team write in a related research article: ãThe key inequality question is therefore not only ãwho will AI replace?ã but also ãwho has the time, trust, training, and work conditions to make AI useful?ãããHow Creative Workers Are Impacted by AIã (2026) was directed by Jan Oliver Lucks and produced by theô Strategic National Arts Alumni Project (SNAAP), a nonprofitô headquarteredô at the College of Fine Arts at the University of Texas at Austin.
                  
                      
                        Film
                      
                      
                        Documentary
                      
                      
                        AI

title: Historic Brooklyn Church Destroyed by ãDevastatingã Fire
description:
News
                The South Bushwick Community Churchãs signature octagonal spire collapsed during a three-alarm blaze on Fri
body:
News
                The South Bushwick Community Churchãs signature octagonal spire collapsed during a three-alarm blaze on Friday.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            South Bushwick Community Church after damage from a fire last Friday, June 19 (photos used with permission)
    
        
            Brooklyn's South Bushwick Community Church is seeking to raise $2 million after a three-alarm blaze caused devastating damage on Friday, June 19. The 173-year-old  Dutch Reformed church caught fire around 1:30 pm on Friday and continued to burn for hours afterward, a New York Fire Department spokesperson told Hyperallergic. The cause of the incident is still under investigation.FDNY members responded to a 3-alarm fire at a church on Bushwick Avenue in Brooklyn on Friday afternoon. Units arrived within three minutes of receiving the call and quickly encountered a heavy fire condition. Within 20 minutes, the incident escalated to a third alarm, bringing aãÎ pic.twitter.com/H1RhG38ky4ã FDNY (@FDNY) June 20, 2026
Videos taken during the event captured the church's signature octagonal spire collapsing into the flames. Its steeple was also destroyed, and the flames reduced some sections of the church, including its facade, to its bare wooden framing. When Hyperallergic visited the site on Monday, June 22, a green fence surrounded the hollowed-out structure.New York City landmarked the church in 1968 for its aesthetic and historical character, including its late Greek Revival architecture. A green fence surrounds the burned structure.In a GoFundMe created by the church, its pastor, Reverend James E. Steward II, asked for donations to fund a "temporary worship space" and restoration efforts, including engineering reviews. So far, the campaign has raised $7,052 in donations toward a $ 2 million goal. ãSouth Bushwick Church has served as more than a building,ã Steward said in his appeal. ãIt has been a home for worship, prayer, youth programs, community outreach, food assistance, pastoral care, and countless life-changing moments.ãBrooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso took to social media to encourage community members to donate to the church's fundraiser. According to local outlets, New York Attorney General Letitia James also visited the church on Friday, delivering optimistic remarks about the congregation's future. ãThis is a landmark, so that means they are getting grants, they will get funding, this will be rebuilt,ã she told Brooklyn News 12. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Brooklyn

schema.headline: Historic Brooklyn Church Destroyed by ãDevastatingã Fire
